translations.translation:
Zheng Hun (Wengong)
鄭渾 (文公)
Lived AD ?–ca.230
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 16 (Wei 16)
byZL181
onMarch 12th, 2025

Zheng Hun, styledWengong, was fromKaifeng,Henan. His great-grandfather was [Zheng] Zhong, and the father of [Zheng] Zhong was [Zheng] Xing, both of whom were famous Ruist scholars.AThe older brother of[Zheng] Hunwas[Zheng] Tai, who plotted withXun You, among others, to executeDong Zhuo. He served asInspectorofYang province, thendied.B
TheBook of Continued Hanstates: [Zheng] Xing was styled Shaogan, and he wasGrandee Remonstrant. [Zheng] Zhong was styled Zishi, and he wasMinister of Finance.
TheAnnals of HanbyZhang Fanstates:[Zheng] Taiwas styledGongye. When he was young, he had talent and had many clever strategies. He knew the world would fall into chaos, so he secretly bonded with the mighty. His family was abundant in wealth, having four hundred hectare fields, yet he often did not have enough food.1Thus, he was renowned in theShandong Region. He was nominated asFilial and Incorrupt, recruited to the offices of theThree [Excellencies]with the chariot of anExcellencycoming to summon him, all of which he did not accept. WhenHe Jinassisted the government, he summoned and employed famous scholars. He had[Zheng] Taiserve asAttending Gentleman of the Secretariatin addition to beingCommandant who Receives the Chariot. When[He] Jinwas about to executePersonnel of the Yellow Gates, he wanted to summonDong Zhuofor as assistance.[Zheng] Taitold[He] Jin, “Dong Zhuois brutish and wicked, and his covetous ambitions cannot be satiated. If he is lent control of court governance and entrusted with major affairs, he will act unrestrained with all his heart, thereby endangering the imperial court.Your Eminent Lordship, with your mighty virtue and your position as a chief regent, you can persevere in your intentions and make your own decisions. Purge the guilty! Do not wait for[Dong] Zhuoto provide aid! Moreover, if this matter is stalled, changes will happen, and past lessons of such2need not be sought afar.” He also explained the necessary tasks of the time, but[He] Jindid not use him. Thus, he abandoned office and left. He toldXun YouofYingchuan, “Lord Heis not easy to assist.” Soon,[He] Jinwas killed, and[Dong] Zhuoindeed seized power, deposing theEmperor. When theGuandong Regiontroops rose up in righteous rebellion,[Dong] Zhuodiscussed sending out a large army. The officials all feared[Dong] Zhuo, and none dared disobey his will.[Zheng] Taiwas intimidated by his might, that it would become increasingly harder to control, so he said, “Governance lies in virtue, not in armies.[Dong] Zhuowas displeased, saying, “If that is so, does the army have no benefit?” Of the masses, there were none that did not change their demeanor, and they were fearful and shivering for[Zheng] Tai.[Zheng] Taithen used sly rhetoric to reply,saying:•••
The implication is that he gave the food to those who needed it.↩
This is referencingDou Wu, who held similar power toHe Jinat the apex of his career. His slow response in executing a plan to kill the eunuchs led to it failing and resulting in his death.↩
“It is not that there is no benefit, but that theShandong Regionis not worth attacking. Right now, theShandong Regionis talking about raising troops in rebellion, that the provinces and commanderies would move through each other, and that the people and armies would encourage each other. That is not impossible. However, since the time of theGlorious and Martial[Emperor], the central empire has been without bedlam, without the noises of chicken cawing and dogs barking. The common people have forgotten about war for a long time.Zhongnionce said, ‘To lead untrained people to war can be said to be discarding them.’ Although they are many, they cannot do any harm. This is the first point.
“Your Eminent Lordship, you came from the western provinces. You served as a general for the country from a young age, so you are experienced in military affairs and have often been on the battlefield. Your reputation is commended throughout the world. Thus, you have shown your might to the common people, and the common people intend to submit to you out of awe. This is the second point.
“YuanBenchuis a younger relative ofExcellenciesandMinisters, born in the capital city, and brought up by women.3ZhangMengzhuois a magnate fromDongping, and he sits upright and does not look around the hall.4KongGongxuis capable of having pure discussion and lofty debates, exhaling into the withered [to make them live] and blowing into the living [to make them wither].5All of them lack the ability to command armies and they are unaccustomed to the harsh and difficult conditions. In being in front of the vanguard, like walking on blades, and decisively battling their foe for victory or death, none of them are your equal,Your Eminent Lordship. This is the thirdpoint.•••
Here, “brought up by women” [體長婦人] is used to imply that becauseYuan Shaowas raised by women in a mansion, he has no understanding of the realities outside of it, which synergizes with the remark about him being born in the capital.↩
Here, “sits upright and does not look around the hall” [坐不窺堂] can be a metaphor for a respectful person who is focused and does not dare look elsewhere, but in this case, it is a metaphor to say thatZhang Miaois single-minded and unable to grasp the greater picture.↩
This is implying thatKong Zhoutalks idealistically and unrealistically even though it may sound convincing.↩
“Examining theShandong Regionwarriors, if there are those who are capable of riding horses, drawing bows, equaling Meng Ben in valor,6matching Qingji in victories,7possessing sincerity like the defense ofLiaocheng,8and having strategy like the plans devised by[Zhang] Liangand[Chen] Ping, then if they have been assigned to be flank commanders responsible for achieving victory, I have not heard about such people. This is the fourthpoint.9•••
Meng Ben was a general during the Warring States. He was said to have feared nothing, not even the fiercest animals.↩
Qingji, son of the King of Wu, was renowned in his time for his bravery and strength.↩
The Yan general defending the city committed suicide to prove his loyalty, rather than die escaping or be disgraced by surrendering.↩
These two strategists were foundational in building theHan dynasty.↩
“Even if there are such people, there have been no royal titles given and there is no hierarchy established. If each of them relies on their armies and their strength, this will lead to everyone being at a stalemate as is in Go, observing the victory and defeat [of each other] and being unwilling to be of the same heart when should move forward as one. This is the fifth point.
“The commanderies of theGuanxi regionare adjacent toShangdang,Taiyuan,Pingyi,Fufeng,Anding, and ever since recent times, we have skirmished with the Hu barbarians. If even our women carry shields, grasp spears, and have bows with arrows in them, need I say more about our valiant warriors too? With them, we will face against the people of theShandong Region, who have forgotten about fight and can be likened as spurring sheep against tigers and wolves. Our victory is ensured. This is the sixth point.
“What is more, of the powerful and the valiant of the world, there is presently none that surpass theBing, theLiang, the Xiongnu, the Tuge, the Huangzhong, the Yicong, and the western Qiang [warriors]. They have always been feared and submitted to by the common people, andYour Eminent Lordshiphas them as your claws and teeth.10If they inflict terror on strong men, need more be said of them on petty villains? This is the seventhpoint.•••
This is a metaphor. “Claws and teeth” [爪牙] refer to military generals.↩
“In addition, the generals and commanders ofYour Eminent Lordshipare all intimate and close confidants, and they have been interacting with you for a long time. Ever since Sanyuan and Xiakou,11their grace and trustworthiness have been highly evident. With their loyalty and sincerity, they can be tasked with going afar, and with their intelligence and strategies, they can be given special instructions. With them, we will face against theShandong Regionand unravel their absurdity. There really is no comparison. This is the eightpoint.•••
This refers to whenDong Zhuoescaped from Qiang territory at Wangyuan with his army intact.↩
“Warfare has three vices: to attack order with chaos is a vice, to attack good with evil is a vice, and to attack obedience with disobedience is a vice. Right now,Your Eminent Lordshiphas governed the empire with fairness, exterminated the wicked eunuchs, and accomplished loyal and righteous deeds. When these three virtues deal with the three vices, then when you solemnly take your leave to vanquish the guilty, who would dare resist? This is the ninth point.
“ZhengKangchengof the eastern provinces has extensively studied the past and the present. He is someone that Ruist students would gather around.BingGenjuofBeihaiis incorrupt, noble, upright, and sincere. He is an exemplar for scholars. If the generals there consult them about their plans and strategies, then according to proofread books about the strong and the weak, Yan, Zhao, Qi, and Liang were not weak, but they were ultimately exterminated byQin; likewise, Wu and Chu of the Seven Princedoms were not few,12but they did not dare go pastXingyang. Moreover, in current times, with the majesty of a virtuous government and the brilliance of capable advisers, if there are those who want to foster chaos to encourage the wicked, they would certainly not agree with each other and accomplish their evil plots. This is the tenth point. If the ten points are adopted even in the smallest form, then to muster troops with no cause will scare the world and to force people who despise military service to congregate is improper. By abandoning your virtue and relying on the army, you would reduce yourauthority.”•••
The Seven Princedoms takingXingyangwould have been a critical blow to theHan dynasty, but they did not attempt to take it, leading to their rebellion failing.↩
[Dong] Zhuowas pleased and made[Zheng] Taia General, giving him command of the armies that would attack theGuandong Region. However, someone told[Dong] Zhuo, “Zheng Taipossesses intelligence and sagacity that transcends over others. However, he is joining in plots with theShandong Region. Now that you have supplied him troops and horses, he will join their clique. This is something you dread,Your Eminent Lordship.”[Dong] Zhuothus confiscated his troops and horses, and had him remain, conferring him asGentleman Consultant. Later, he andWang Yunplotted together to execute[Dong] Zhuo. He escaped and fled out ofWu pass, joining the east. TheGeneral of the Rear,Yuan Shu, appointed him to serve asInspectorofYang province, but he did not arrive at his office, for he died along the way. He was forty-two at the time.
[Zheng] Hunled the young son of[Zheng] Tai,[Zheng] Mao, toHuainanto evade danger.Yuan Shutreated them extremely generously and with respect accorded to guests.[Zheng] Hunknew[Yuan] Shuwould eventually fall. At the time,Hua Xinserved asGrand AdministratorofYuzhang, and he had always been friendly with[Zheng] Tai, and thus,[Zheng] Hunferried across theYangzi Riverto join[Hua] Xin. TheGrand Progenitorheard about his sincere conduct and summoned him to serve as abureaucrat. He was then promoted toChiefofXiacai, thenPrefectofShaoling. The world had not been stabilized yet, and so the people were all impetuous and undisciplined, not giving deep consideration to reproducing. Any born children with nothing to be given for their survival were all not raised. Wherever[Zheng] Hunwas, he would seize tools for fishing and hunting, and he tasked [people] with doing agriculture and sericulture. He also opened up rice paddies and made the law for abandoning children more severe. The people were initially afraid of punishments, but later, they gradually became more wealthy such that there were none who could not raise and supply [their children]. Many of the raised sons and daughters made “Zheng” a part of their name.
He was recruited to be abureaucratic clerkunder theAssisting Chancellor, then was promoted to serve inZuopingyi. At the time,Liang Xing, among others, raided over five thousand official and peasant families to steal. The other counties could not resist and they were all terrified, so the commandery seat was to be temporarily moved. Debaters all believed that it was prudent to move to a strategic fortress, but[Zheng] Hunsaid, “[Liang] Xingand his ilk have been defeated and scattered, hiding in the mountains. Although they have followers, most are forced to obey. What we should do now is broaden the path for surrender and proclaim our kindness and sincerity. To guard a strategic pass to preserve ourselves shows weakness.” He then gathered the people to construct inner and outer city walls to serve as sturdy defenses. He dispatched people to pursue the bandits, making the rewards and punishments clear, and swore with them that they would receive seven-tenths of what they captured as a reward. The common people were very pleased and they were all willing to seize bandits. Many of them captured wives, daughters, money, and property. The bandits who lost their wives and children all came back and asked to surrender.[Zheng] Hundemanded that they capture the wives and daughters of the others for their wives and children to be returned. From this, they were robbing each other, and their clique became more divided. Officials who had kindness and sincerity were also sent all over the ravines to make proclamations [to persuade their surrender]. There were many who came out [to surrender] in succession, so the senior officials of the counties were ordered to return to their original government seats to appease and organize them.[Liang] Xingand the others were afraid, and so they led their remaining troops to gather atFucheng. When theGrand ProgenitorsentXiahou Yuanto help the commandery in attack them,[Zheng] Hunled officials and peasants in advancing forward, beheading[Liang] Xingand his comrades.
There was also a bandit,Jin Fu, who, among others, coerced theChiefofXiayang, thePrefectofShaoling, and their officials and peasants into entering Wei mountain.[Zheng] Hunattacked and defeated[Jin] Fuand his ilk, receiving the senior officials of the two counties and leading the coerced to return.
There was also aZhao Qinglongwho killed Left Inner CensorCheng Xiu, and when[Zheng] Hunheard about him, he sent strong warriors to behead and hang his head. From beginning to end, over four thousand families joined and submitted, and from this, the mountain bandits were all pacified and the peasants were safe to do their work. He was transferred to becomeGrand AdministratorofShangdang.
When theGrand ProgenitorconqueredHanzhong, he made[Zheng] Hunserve asIntendantofJingzhao.[Zheng] Hunconsidered that the common people had just recently gathered, so he established laws for shifting residences. He tasked both extensive families and single families to pair with each other, and tasked gentle and trustworthy people to pair with solitary elders, then had them work hard to sow and reap. He made prohibitions coherent so as to expose evildoers. From this, the peasants felt safe doing agriculture and the bandits ceased [their activities]. When the main army enteredHanzhong,[Zheng] Hundid the best at transporting military provisions. He also sent people to farm inHanzhong, and there was no one who fled. TheGrand Progenitorincreasingly admired him and had him enter [the central government] again as abureaucratunder theAssisting Chancellor.
After theCivil Emperorassumed the throne, he becameImperial Clerk, in addition to beingCommandant of Escorting Cavalry. He was promoted to being theGrand AdministratorofYangpingthenGrand AdministratorofPei. The commanderies were low and humid, and suffered from flooding due to rain, causing the common people to be famished and lacking. When[Zheng] Hunwas inXiaoandXiangcounties, he built reservoirs and opened up rice paddies. The commandery people all thought this was not feasible, but[Zheng] Hunsaid, “The geography of the land is low-lying and it would be practicable to irrigate. Eventually, there will be improvements to fishing and rice farming that will last for a very long time. This is the foundation for enriching the people!” He then personally led the officials and peasants to engineer, and within the span of one winter, everything was completed. With every year, there would be a great harvest. The farmlands and fields would expand with each year and the income of the land taxes would be double the average. Because the people relied on his improvements, they carved an eulogy for him on stone, titling it “Zheng Reservoir”. He was transferred to becomeGrand AdministratorofShanyangthenGrand AdministratorofWei commandery, and his governance in them was as such. The common people of the commandery were distressed by the lack of timber, so they were directed to cultivate elm trees to build fences and to also cultivate five types of fruit.13The elm trees were all made into fences and the five types of fruits were abundant in fruit. Thus, when one enteredWei commandery, the villages were all organized and uniform, and the people were wealthy and abundant. When theBrightEmperorheard about this, he issued an edict praising him, and announced it across the realm under heaven. He was promoted toCourt Architect.
[Zheng] Hunwas always incorrupt and pure in his duties, and his wife and children were not exempt from being famished and poor. When he died, his son,[Zheng] Chong, becamePalace Gentleman.C•••
The five types of fruits generally refer to peach, plum, apricot, chestnut and jujube.↩
TheChronicles of Jinstates: The son of[Zheng] Taiwas[Zheng] Mao, styledLinshu.[Zheng] Taiwas friendly withHua XinandXun You. When they met[Zheng] Mao, they said, “ZhengGongyehas not died!” He first served as a literary adviser to theMarquisofLinzi, then was promoted to beBrilliantly Blessed Grandee. In the seventh year of theera [271],[Zheng] Maowas made to beExcellency of Works, but he firmly declined and did not receive it. He died at his household. His son, [Zheng] Mo, was styled Siyuan.
TheAppraisal of the Lords of Jinstates: [Zheng] Mo respectfully maintained the familial estate, and he was praised for his sincerity and purity. He position reachedMinister of Ceremonies. The younger brothers of [Zheng] Mo were [Zheng] Zan, [Zheng] Shu, and [Zheng] Xu, all of whom were madeMinisters. The son of [Zheng] Mo was [Zheng] Qiu, who was incorrupt and honest, possessing logic and insight. He served as RightVice Secretariat Minister, directing selections [of officials]. The younger brother of [Zheng] Qiu was [Zheng] Yu, who served asSecretariat Minister.
ㅤ
